Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed infinitival verb phrase with object complement; figurative transitive construction built on kushiya plus locative body-part phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To keep a grudge or a heavy worry in one’s mind instead of speaking openly.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kupeta dibanza dia bululu mu mutshima ne mu meji.
Usage Contexts: family disputes; reconciliation talks; community mediation; advice from elders
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Linked to family-palaver traditions where unresolved resentment is described as a burden carried on the head, a visible place of load-bearing in daily life.
First Example Sentence: Kadi kuikala ushiyi nkuadi ku mutu, ambila mamu malu aku bimpe.
Second Example Sentence: Bantu ba mu dibota bakenza disambila bua kabayi bashiya nkuadi ku mutu.
Third Example Sentence: Mukulu wakamba ne: muntu udi ushiyi nkuadi ku mutu kena upeta ditalala.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Subject–verb–object clause with personified abstract noun; progressive aspect marking on verb udia.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Anxiety, envy, or fear is quietly consuming someone from within.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Udi ne tshikondo tshia bowa anyi tshia lulengu lua munda.
Usage Contexts: emotional counseling; family observation; literature; jealousy; private worry
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on the Luba-Kasai view of mutshima as the center of feeling, conscience, courage, and relational balance.
First Example Sentence: Kubanga mukaji wende wakaya, moyo udi udia mutshima wa Kalala butuku ne muinshi.
Second Example Sentence: Mwana udi useka, kadi moyo udi udia mutshima chifukwa cha malu a kalasa.
Third Example Sentence: Paakumona mukanda wa musangu eu, moyo wakadi udia mutshima wende bikole.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival motion verb phrase with locative prepositional complement; metaphorical place expression.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To reach the hidden truth or the root cause of a difficult matter.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kumanya tshisamba tshia malu anyi ntuadijilu wa kanyinganyinga.
Usage Contexts: investigation; problem-solving; family councils; workplace review; teaching
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: References stones used as markers or coverings; what lies under the stone is not immediately visible and must be uncovered carefully.
First Example Sentence: Tudi ne bua kufika muinshi mua dibue tuetu bonso tuetuikale ne bushuwa.
Second Example Sentence: Mulombodi kena ufua moyo to ne afike muinshi mua dibue mu mudimu eu.
Third Example Sentence: Bikalayi tumona bipendu bia bantu, katuena tufiki muinshi mua dibue.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival verb phrase with locative complement; animal-body metaphor with possessive noun phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To follow a powerful person too closely, depending on their influence instead of acting independently.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kulonda muntu wa bukole bua kupeta dianyisha anyi bujitu.
Usage Contexts: politics; workplace hierarchy; patronage; satire; social commentary
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The elephant symbolizes strength and status; walking by its tail suggests dependence on borrowed power and risky proximity.
First Example Sentence: Mu tshisalu tshia politiki, badi bangi bayoya mu mukila wa nzolu.
Second Example Sentence: Tatu wakamuseka ne udi uyoya mu mukila wa nzolu panzubu wa bakokeshi.
Third Example Sentence: Muntu udi uyoya mu mukila wa nzolu kena umanya buimpe buandi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal subject plus existential verb phrase; personification with plural noun and possessed body part.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A situation is dangerous, delicate, or likely to hurt someone if handled carelessly.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Malu aa adi makole ne adi mua kuleta tshinyangu.
Usage Contexts: warnings; legal disputes; business negotiations; family conflicts
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses teeth as a familiar image of harm, warning listeners that a matter can bite like a living creature.
First Example Sentence: Kadi kuela tshisumbu mu nsangilu eu, malu adi ne mino.
Second Example Sentence: Badi balombangana bua budimi bua bakulu, malu adi ne mino penyi.
Third Example Sentence: Mulongeshi wakambila bana ne malu a maluisha adi ne mino.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival transitive verb phrase with locative complement; action metaphor involving water and a deep/long passage.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To waste effort, resources, or advice where it will not produce results.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kuenza mudimu anyi kupesha dianyisha pa tshintu tshidi katshiena ntema.
Usage Contexts: work planning; teaching; parenting; business; advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Evokes the futility of pouring valuable water into a place where it disappears, reflecting the practical value of water in household life.
First Example Sentence: Kubuela mu mudimu kawena mua kupangila, nku kukanda mayi mu lubila.
Second Example Sentence: Mamu wakamba ne kumulaya musangu yonso kudi kukanda mayi mu lubila.
Third Example Sentence: Bikale bena mudimu kabena bumvua, mayele etu adi akandibua mu lubila.

Idiom (Luba-Kasai): Dimi didi dibuela ku meji kumpala
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Subject–progressive verb–locative complement clause; body-part subject with temporal adverbial.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone speaks before thinking and may cause trouble through careless words.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Udi wamba malu kabidi kayi abanza bimpe.
Usage Contexts: youth guidance; family correction; public speaking; conflict prevention
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects communal emphasis on measured speech, respect, and listening before responding in councils and family gatherings.
First Example Sentence: Dimi diaku didi dibuela ku meji kumpala, nanku wakapanga bena nsangilu.
Second Example Sentence: Mulunda wanyi udi ne mutshima muimpe, kadi dimi diandi didi dibuela ku meji kumpala.
Third Example Sentence: Mu diku, bakulu badi balaya bana bua dimi kadibueli ku meji kumpala.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival verb phrase with object complement; visual perception metaphor lexicalized as idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To judge by appearance only without understanding the deeper reality.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kutangila tshintu pa mesu mpamba kayi umanya bushuwa buadi.
Usage Contexts: marketplace; relationships; moral teaching; product reviews; social judgment
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Connects seeing and eating to hasty judgment, warning against being deceived by surfaces in trade and social life.
First Example Sentence: Kadi kudia tshia ku mesu, nzubu eu udi ukenza malu a bungi.
Second Example Sentence: Bamvuala badi bapesha muntu bujitu, kadi kudia tshia ku mesu kudi kupambuisha.
Third Example Sentence: Mu tshisalu, muntu udi udia tshia ku mesu upanga kushala ne lukasa.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival stative verb phrase with locative prepositional complement; symbolic object phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To live or act with fragile prestige that can vanish quickly under pressure.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kuikala ne bujitu budi kabuyi bukole anyi kabuyi buimana.
Usage Contexts: literature; politics; social criticism; leadership; ceremonial speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Lupemba, white ritual chalk/clay associated with purity, honor, and ceremony, suggests prestige that is bright yet easily disturbed.
First Example Sentence: Mulombodi udi ulaya malu kayi uenza udi usomba pa mukuna wa lupemba.
Second Example Sentence: Bujitu buakapeta mu nsangilu buakadi buimpe, kadi wakasomba pa mukuna wa lupemba.
Third Example Sentence: Mu mukanda wa mufundi, mfumu wakadi usomba pa mukuna wa lupemba.

Idiom (Luba-Kasai): Kukuba lukasa ku malu adi alala
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival transitive verb phrase with object clause; causative/agitative metaphor using lukasa as acceleration.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To revive a settled conflict or provoke a problem that had calmed down.
Usage Contexts: family peacekeeping; politics; online arguments; mediation; gossip
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Grounded in reconciliation culture: once a matter has slept, disturbing it is seen as socially dangerous and disrespectful.
First Example Sentence: Kadi kukuba lukasa ku malu adi alala, dibota diakatala kale ditalala.
Second Example Sentence: Mu nsangilu, mukulu wakamupuekesha bua wakakuba lukasa ku malu adi alala.
Third Example Sentence: Meyi a pa nzubu a pa mukanda wa intaneti adi akuba lukasa ku malu adi alala.
